review.body:
## Pull request overview

Reverts the change that made settings experiment-aware by default by reintroducing an explicit opt-in model for experiment-controlled configuration defaults across the workbench and core configuration registry.

**Changes:**
- Replace `experimentMode` with an explicit `experiment` object (`mode` + optional `name`) on configuration property schemas and update the default-override contribution logic accordingly.
- Update a set of built-in settings to explicitly opt into experiments via `experiment: { mode: ... }` (and migrate existing startup-only experiment settings).
- Remove the now-obsolete test suite that validated “experiment-aware by default” behavior.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 17 out of 17 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| src/vs/workbench/services/configuration/test/browser/configurationDefaultOverrides.test.ts | Removes tests that assumed experiment overrides apply by default for settings without explicit experiment metadata. |
| src/vs/workbench/services/configuration/browser/configurationService.ts | Updates default override contribution to only process settings with `schema.experiment` and to honor `mode`/`name`. |
| src/vs/workbench/contrib/welcomeGettingStarted/browser/gettingStarted.contribution.ts | Marks `workbench.startupEditor` as experiment-enabled (`auto`). |
| src/vs/workbench/contrib/terminalContrib/chatAgentTools/common/terminalChatAgentToolsConfiguration.ts | Explicitly opts several terminal chat agent tool settings into experiments (`auto`). |
| src/vs/workbench/contrib/terminal/common/terminalConfiguration.ts | Explicitly opts several terminal settings into experiments (`auto`). |
| src/vs/workbench/contrib/performance/electron-browser/performance.contribution.ts | Migrates a startup experiment setting from `experimentMode` to `experiment: { mode: 'startup' }`. |
| src/vs/workbench/contrib/inlineChat/common/inlineChat.ts | Adds `experiment` metadata to multiple inline chat settings; migrates startup mode where applicable. |
| src/vs/workbench/contrib/extensions/browser/extensions.contribution.ts | Marks an extensions setting as experiment-enabled (`auto`). |
| src/vs/workbench/contrib/editTelemetry/browser/editTelemetry.contribution.ts | Marks edit telemetry settings as experiment-enabled (`auto`). |
| src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/chat.contribution.ts | Migrates startup experiment settings and opts additional chat settings into experiments (`auto`). |
| src/vs/workbench/contrib/browserView/electron-browser/browserView.contribution.ts | Migrates a startup experiment setting to the new `experiment` object. |
| src/vs/workbench/browser/workbench.contribution.ts | Marks selected workbench settings as experiment-enabled (`auto`). |
| src/vs/workbench/api/common/configurationExtensionPoint.ts | Changes extension-contributed experiment opt-in behavior to depend on the `onExP` tag and set `experiment`. |
| src/vs/platform/request/common/request.ts | Marks select proxy-related settings as experiment-enabled (`auto`). |
| src/vs/platform/configuration/common/configurationRegistry.ts | Introduces `experiment` schema property, updates `onExP` tag handling, and updates validation messaging. |
| src/vs/editor/common/config/editorOptions.ts | Marks several editor settings as experiment-enabled (`auto`). |
| src/vs/editor/common/config/editorConfigurationSchema.ts | Marks additional editor configuration properties as experiment-enabled (`auto`). |
</details>

comment.body: The experiment default override logic now depends on `schema.experiment` and uses `experiment.mode`/`experiment.name`, but there doesn’t appear to be remaining unit test coverage for this behavior (the prior dedicated test file was removed, and there are no other tests exercising this flow). Please add/update tests to cover applying an experiment override, skipping when treatment is undefined or equals the default, and the difference between `mode: 'auto'` (re-applies on refetch) vs `mode: 'startup'` (does not).

issue.title: Monitor the current TypeScript context provider experiment

labels.name: plan-item
labels.description: VS Code - planned item for upcoming

milestone.title: On Deck
milestone.description: The Issues assigned to the **On Deck** milestone under consideration for the upcoming milestones.

issue.body: Especially try to understand why we still see different acceptance rate for different platforms. For Window it seems to be a lot lower than for Linux and MacOS.

comment.body: Evaluating the metrics for the TS context provider turned out to be very difficult for the following reasons: - treatment and control groups can't be defined by language type - we can post filter the score cards however this led to misbalanced treatment and control groups is some cases - Score card run for a longer period of time started to fail with OOM exceptions. This is mainly because there are so many end of life events - the TS context provider is basically a power user feature since it requires and proper TS setup (e.g. tsconfig.json file) - it is only useful in workspaces that is primarily TypeScript and has a fair amount of TS files. I therefore tried to compute the key metrics and survival rate over the RAW VSCode events. However this only allowed to inspect ~2500 devices which didn't result in a large enough population to compute a meaning full values. I then created flat tables for the end of life, inline edit arc and typescript context provider events and backfilled the table with data from the experiment that we ran in Feb. I also limited the metrics to values that indicated a TS workspace. In detail I did the following: - considered [DevDeviceId, SessionId] pairs where 50% of the end of life events were for TS files in both the control and treatment group. The comparison factor of the two groups is 0.9851 indicating that the experiment itself didn't have an impact on the treatment group. - further limited the treatment group to workspaces where at least 20% of the context requests returned at least 1 snippet. Producing a snippet indicates that the TS project is very likely setup correctly and that a TS server version is used that is supported by the TS context provider. Numbers from the context provider further show that in average only max 30% of the request don't produce a snippet *Side Note*: what is interesting as well is that on weekends the zero snippets increase which indicates that on the weekend we have more hobby programmers with a suboptimal TS project setup. Bases on these [DevDeviceId, SessionId] pairs from the control and treatment group I then computed the key metrics and survial rates. The result numbers (as screen shots) are:

issue.body:
## Summary

Add a new VS Code setting `dev.containers.defaultMounts` that allows users to specify personal mount paths that should be automatically added to devcontainers, similar to how `dev.containers.defaultFeatures` works for features.

## Problem

When working with devcontainers, developers often need access to personal folders from the host OS (like `.git`, `.ssh`, `.codeium`, etc.) that shouldn't be included in the shared `devcontainer.json` file since it's generic and used by the entire team.

Currently, the only way to mount these personal paths is to modify the `devcontainer.json` directly, which:
- Pollutes the shared configuration with personal settings
- Requires manual modification for each project
- Can accidentally be committed to version control

## Proposed Solution

Add a new VS Code setting `dev.containers.defaultMounts` that works similarly to `dev.containers.defaultFeatures`. This setting would allow users to specify mount paths in their personal VS Code settings that get automatically applied to all devcontainers.

### Example Configuration

```json
{
  "dev.containers.defaultMounts": [
    "source=${localEnv:HOME}/.ssh,target=/home/vscode/.ssh,type=bind,consistency=cached",
    "source=${localEnv:HOME}/.gitconfig,target=/home/vscode/.gitconfig,type=bind,consistency=cached",
    "source=${localEnv:HOME}/.codeium,target=/home/vscode/.codeium,type=bind,consistency=cached"
  ]
}
```

### Benefits

1. **Personal Configuration**: Users can maintain their personal mount requirements without affecting shared devcontainer configurations
2. **Consistency**: Same personal mounts applied across all projects automatically
3. **Clean Separation**: Keeps shared devcontainer.json clean and focused on project-specific requirements
4. **Follows Existing Pattern**: Mirrors the successful `dev.containers.defaultFeatures` approach

## Implementation Notes

- The default mounts should be merged with any mounts specified in the `devcontainer.json`
- Should support the same mount syntax as the existing `mounts` property
- Should respect the same environment variable substitution (like `${localEnv:HOME}`)

This would greatly improve the developer experience when working with devcontainers by allowing seamless integration of personal development tools and configurations.

comment.body: I have to remove all mounts from my devcontainers.json because of codespaces and gitpod. Moreover it's not secure at all to serve such a container requesting folders outside of workspace . So moving it to personal settings would make it way more sense

issue.title: Method not found error when using MCP servers that don't support Apps/Widgets
issue.body:
## `MCP -32601: Method not found` error when using MCP servers that don't support Apps/Widgets

**Product:** VS Code + GitHub Copilot Chat
**MCP Server:** Atlassian (via Runlayer gateway)
**Date:** 2026-03-08

---

### Summary

I'm an engineer at Runlayer, one of our customers had an issue with regards to connecting the Atlassian MCP server. This works totally fine in other MCP clients, but seems to be problematic in VSCode Copilot Chat only (with and without Runlayer, an MCP server proxy).

VS Code's MCP client calls `resources/read` with `ui://widget/` URIs (the "MCP Apps" feature) against MCP servers that do not advertise or support this capability. The server correctly returns JSON-RPC error `-32601: Method not found`, but VS Code surfaces this as a disruptive `Error loading MCP App: MPC -32601: Method not found` notification to the user, even though the underlying tool calls succeed.

### Environment

- **VS Code:** 1.110.1
- **MCP Client:** GitHub Copilot Chat (built-in)
- **MCP Transport:** Streamable HTTP
- **MCP Server:** Atlassian (hosted via Runlayer at `tenant.runlayer.com`)

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure an Atlassian MCP server in VS Code using Streamable HTTP transport:
   ```json
   {
     "servers": {
       "atlassian": {
         "type": "http",
         "url": "https://tenant.runlayer.com/api/v1/proxy/<connector-id>/mcp"
            OR
         "url": "https://mcp.atlassian.com/v1/mcp"
       }
     }
   }
   ```
2. Start the MCP server — it initializes successfully and discovers 31 tools
3. In Copilot Chat, ask a question that triggers a Jira tool call (e.g., search for issues using `searchJiraIssuesUsingJql`)
4. Observe the error notification: `Error loading MCP App: MPC -32601: Method not found`

### Expected Behavior

- The tool call should complete without an error notification
- If the server does not support `resources/read` for `ui://widget/*` URIs, the client should silently skip the Apps/Widget feature rather than showing an error to the user
- At minimum, the error should not block or confuse the user since the actual tool call succeeds

### Actual Behavior

- VS Code sends `resources/read` requests with `uri: "ui://widget/jira-widget.html"` to the server
- The Atlassian MCP server does not support this method and returns `{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":8,"error":{"code":-32601,"message":"Method not found"}}`
- VS Code displays a prominent error notification: `Error loading MCP App: MPC -32601: Method not found`
- Despite the error, the actual Jira tool call (`tools/call` → `searchJiraIssuesUsingJql`) succeeds and returns valid results
- The user sees both the error message **and** successful results, which is confusing

### Trace Log Evidence

The trace logs confirm VS Code is calling `resources/read` with widget URIs that the server doesn't support:

```
[editor -> server] {"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":7,"method":"resources/read","params":{"uri":"ui://widget/jira-widget.html"}}
[editor -> server] {"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":8,"method":"resources/read","params":{"uri":"ui://widget/jira-widget.html"}}

[server -> editor] {"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":8,"error":{"code":-32601,"message":"Method not found"}}
[server -> editor] {"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":7,"error":{"code":-32601,"message":"Method not found"}}
```

The tool call that follows works correctly:
```
[editor -> server] {"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":9,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"searchJiraIssuesUsingJql",...}}
```

### Root Cause Analysis

The Atlassian MCP server does not advertise MCP Apps support in its `initialize` response, yet VS Code's MCP client attempts to load `ui://widget/jira-widget.html` resources regardless. Per the MCP specification, a `-32601` response indicates the method is not supported by the server, and the client should handle this gracefully.

### Additional Note

The error message displays `MPC -32601` (note: "MPC" instead of "MCP"), which appears to be a typo in VS Code's error handling code. Reference: [`mcpTypes.ts` L408-L412](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/a0ce4461e866373740e50b4df19cc9176cfe67be/src/vs/workbench/contrib/mcp/common/mcpTypes.ts#L408-L412). This was also reported in [microsoft/vscode#246591](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/246591).

### Impact

- **Severity:** Low (functional — tool calls succeed despite the error)
- **User impact:** High (confusing error message appears on every tool invocation, eroding trust)
- **Workaround:** None currently available client-side. The error is cosmetic and does not prevent actual tool usage.

### Recommended Fix

VS Code's MCP client should:
1. Check the server's advertised capabilities before requesting `ui://widget/*` resources
2. Gracefully handle `-32601` responses for optional features without surfacing errors to the user
3. Fix the "MPC" → "MCP" typo in the error message

comment.body: btw, this does actually work for me

issue.title: Fix PSReadline for screen reader
issue.body: Part of: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/297146 Problem1: * Red error: Import-Module $specialPsrlPath runs without checking if the folder exists. In production builds, the psreadline/ folder isn't there → red error in terminal. * Unreachable `Set-PSReadLineOption` -EnableScreenReaderMode` since pwsh 7.5+ loaded stripped down version of psreadline 2.3.6. which gave the `"WARNING: PowerShell detected that you might be using a screen reader and has disabled PSReadLine as it may not work correctly with your screen reader. If you want to re-enable it, run 'Import-Module PSReadLine'."` We need the bundled 2.3.4-beta3 that has the `EnableScreenReaderMode` param. `EnableScreenReaderMode ` is available starting PS ReadLine 2.4.3+ which is more screen reader friendly. Pre-PR, the code was prevented from setting `Set-PSReadLineOption EnableScreenReaderMode ` Problem 2: Bundled PSReadLine not shipped in production builds Symptom: Import-Module threw a red error in production VS Code because the psreadline/ folder didn't exist — it was only present in dev builds?? * Fix should be editing gulpfiles also adding Test-Path guard to be safer. --------------------------- Holding off on below until clarification --> https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/297146#issuecomment-3999672402 Problem 3: CopyRight banner * Use `-nologo ` when accessibility mode enabled.

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

As vscode extension author who develop extensions to provide agentic solution to the vscode copilot users, I would like to be able to install the plugin from the desired location to enhance user's experience, such as the github repository where the source code of the plugin lives. cc/ @kvenkatrajan @charris-msft

issue.title: Extension-contributed HTTP MCP servers cannot auto-start -- no way to mark as required or force auth prompt

labels.name: *as-designed
labels.description: Described behavior is as designed

issue.body:
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates -->
<!-- Please attach logs to help us diagnose your issue -->

- Copilot Chat Extension Version: 0.39.0
- VS Code Version: 1.111.0 (Insiders)
- OS Version: Windows 11
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): MCP server auto-start / tool visibility
- Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): N/A (applies to all models)
- Logs: N/A (no errors — the issue is behavioral)

## Summary

Extension-contributed HTTP MCP servers that require remote authentication (e.g., Microsoft/GitHub auth) **cannot auto-start**, even when `chat.mcp.autoStart` is set to `newAndOutdated` (the default). There is no API to mark an MCP as required, force the auth prompt, or reuse existing VS Code MSAL credentials.

When a user opens chat, the MCP silently remains unstarted. A small, easy-to-miss inline message appears at the bottom of the first chat response:

> *"The MCP server HTTP_MCP_Repro may have new tools and requires interaction to start. Start it now?"*

If the user doesn't notice this, **chat continues without the MCP tools** — silently degraded with no prominent indication. This creates a confusing point of friction where users won't have access to potentially critical MCPs unless they are actively watching for and responding to a small inline notice at chat start.

**Compounding this**, MCP tools cannot be referenced with `#` notation in chat until the server has been manually started (tracked separately in #272000), making unstarted MCP tools completely invisible from the chat input.

## Reproduction Repository

**Full reproduction extension with screenshots:** https://github.com/KeithIsSleeping/mcp-http-autostart-repro

Steps to Reproduce:

### Prerequisites
- VS Code Insiders 1.111.0+
- GitHub Copilot Chat v0.39.0+
- Signed in to a Microsoft/GitHub account

### Setup
```bash
git clone https://github.com/KeithIsSleeping/mcp-http-autostart-repro
cd mcp-http-autostart-repro
npm install
npm run compile
```

Launch the Extension Development Host:
- Press `F5` in VS Code, **or**
- From terminal: `code-insiders --extensionDevelopmentPath .`

### Issue 1: MCP Does Not Auto-Start

1. In the Extension Development Host window, open any folder
2. Open the Chat view (`Ctrl+Alt+I`)
3. Send a message in chat (e.g., "hello")
4. **Observe:** Chat responds **without** MCP tools. A small message appears at the bottom:
   > *"The MCP server HTTP_MCP_Repro may have new tools and requires interaction to start. Start it now?"*
5. Click the **Configure Tools** button (gear icon in the chat input)
6. **Observe:** Under "HTTP_MCP_Repro", you see **"Update tools"** — the server has not auto-started
7. The MCP **never starts automatically**

| Screenshot | Description |
|---|---|
| ![MCP requires interaction](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KeithIsSleeping/mcp-http-autostart-repro/none/docs/mcp-requires-interaction.png) | Small inline "requires interaction" notice — easy to miss |
| ![Update Tools shown](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KeithIsSleeping/mcp-http-autostart-repro/none/docs/mcp-update-tools.png) | Tools picker shows "Update tools" instead of auto-started tools |

### Issue 2: MCP Tools Not Visible via `#` Until Started

1. With the MCP still unstarted (do not click "Start it now?")
2. In the chat input, type `#` to open the tool/reference picker
3. **Observe:** No tools from "HTTP_MCP_Repro" appear
4. Now click "Start it now?" to start the MCP and complete auth
5. Type `#` again — the MCP tools now appear

| Screenshot | Description |
|---|---|
| ![No MCP reference](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KeithIsSleeping/mcp-http-autostart-repro/none/docs/no-mcp-reference.png) | MCP tools invisible via `#` until manually started |

### Alternative: `.vscode/mcp.json` (same behavior)

```json
{
    "servers": {
        "HTTP_MCP_Test": {
            "type": "http",
            "url": "https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp"
        }
    }
}
```

The same behavior occurs — the server requires interaction to start.

## Expected Behavior

Extension-contributed HTTP MCP servers should either:
1. **Auto-start and trigger the auth prompt prominently** (modal dialog or banner), OR
2. **Provide an `autoStart` or `required` property on `McpServerDefinition`** so extensions can declare that their MCP should auto-start or block chat until authorized, OR
3. **Provide a public API to start an MCP** (e.g., `vscode.lm.startMcpServer(serverId)`) so extensions can trigger the auth flow programmatically, OR
4. **Honor pre-cached auth sessions** — if a valid MSAL/Microsoft auth session exists (from `vscode.authentication.getSession`), allow the MCP to auto-connect without additional interaction

## Actual Behavior

- The MCP never auto-starts regardless of `chat.mcp.autoStart` setting
- Chat silently continues without MCP tools
- The only indication is a small inline message that most users will miss
- MCP tools are invisible via `#` in chat until manually started
- No API exists to mark an MCP as required, trigger the auth flow, or reuse cached credentials

## Impact

Extensions shipping HTTP MCP servers to large user bases have **no way to ensure the MCP is available when the user starts chatting**. The current UX silently falls back to chat without the tools. Most users never realize they need to click a small link to activate them. For extensions providing critical domain-specific tools via MCP, this means users get degraded responses without understanding why.

## Related Issues

- #275919 — MCP tools registered by extension requires "Update tools" action (closed)
- #272000 — Proposed API for MCP tool metadata (open, doesn't address auto-start or `#` visibility)

- Signed in to a Microsoft/GitHub account

### Setup
```bash
git clone https://github.com/KeithIsSleeping/mcp-http-autostart-repro
cd mcp-http-autostart-repro
npm install
npm run compile
```

Launch the Extension Development Host:
- Press `F5` in VS Code, **or**
- From terminal: `code-insiders --extensionDevelopmentPath .`

### Issue 1: MCP Does Not Auto-Start

1. In the Extension Development Host window, open any folder
2. Open the Chat view (`Ctrl+Alt+I`)
3. Send a message in chat (e.g., "hello")
4. **Observe:** Chat responds **without** MCP tools. A small message appears at the bottom:
   > *"The MCP server HTTP_MCP_Repro may have new tools and requires interaction to start. Start it now?"*
5. Click the **Configure Tools** button (gear icon in the chat input)
6. **Observe:** Under "HTTP_MCP_Repro", you see **"Update tools"** — the server has not auto-started
7. The MCP **never starts automatically**

| Screenshot | Description |
|---|---|
| ![MCP requires interaction](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KeithIsSleeping/mcp-http-autostart-repro/none/docs/mcp-requires-interaction.png) | Small inline "requires interaction" notice — easy to miss |
| ![Update Tools shown](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KeithIsSleeping/mcp-http-autostart-repro/none/docs/mcp-update-tools.png) | Tools picker shows "Update tools" instead of auto-started tools |

### Issue 2: MCP Tools Not Visible via `#` Until Started

1. With the MCP still unstarted (do not click "Start it now?")
2. In the chat input, type `#` to open the tool/reference picker
3. **Observe:** No tools from "HTTP_MCP_Repro" appear
4. Now click "Start it now?" to start the MCP and complete auth
5. Type `#` again — the MCP tools now appear

| Screenshot | Description |
|---|---|
| ![No MCP reference](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KeithIsSleeping/mcp-http-autostart-repro/none/docs/no-mcp-reference.png) | MCP tools invisible via `#` until manually started |

### Alternative: `.vscode/mcp.json` (same behavior)

```json
{
    "servers": {
        "HTTP_MCP_Test": {
            "type": "http",
            "url": "https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp"
        }
    }
}
```

The same behavior occurs — the server requires interaction to start.

## Expected Behavior

Extension-contributed HTTP MCP servers should either:
1. **Auto-start and trigger the auth prompt prominently** (modal dialog or banner), OR
2. **Provide an `autoStart` or `required` property on `McpServerDefinition`** so extensions can declare that their MCP should auto-start or block chat until authorized, OR
3. **Provide a public API to start an MCP** (e.g., `vscode.lm.startMcpServer(serverId)`) so extensions can trigger the auth flow programmatically, OR
4. **Honor pre-cached auth sessions** — if a valid MSAL/Microsoft auth session exists (from `vscode.authentication.getSession`), allow the MCP to auto-connect without additional interaction

## Actual Behavior

- The MCP never auto-starts regardless of `chat.mcp.autoStart` setting
- Chat silently continues without MCP tools
- The only indication is a small inline message that most users will miss
- MCP tools are invisible via `#` in chat until manually started
- No API exists to mark an MCP as required, trigger the auth flow, or reuse cached credentials

## Impact

Extensions shipping HTTP MCP servers to large user bases have **no way to ensure the MCP is available when the user starts chatting**. The current UX silently falls back to chat without the tools. Most users never realize they need to click a small link to activate them. For extensions providing critical domain-specific tools via MCP, this means users get degraded responses without understanding why.

## Related Issues

- #275919 — MCP tools registered by extension requires "Update tools" action (closed)
- #272000 — Proposed API for MCP tool metadata (open, doesn't address auto-start or `#` visibility)

comment.body: This is quite intentional, but note there is a command available `workbench.mcp.startServer` which will force start all available MCP servers. You can call that from an extension if appropriate

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->
Hi 👋🏻 

Really loving new VS Code updates 🫶 

While writing custom agents for orchestrations, I noticed in cloud documentation, they allow preloading skills for custom agents so that when we run that agent, it already has those skills. Before reading this documentation, I already created a skilled retrieval agent. If VS Code allows preloading skills for custom agent, then I can remove my own agent.

Moreover, it is more beneficial and deterministic approach to building custom agent with set of specific skills.

Reference: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#preload-skills-into-subagents

issue.title: Latest vscode beta breaks vscode-copilot-chat beta due to "proposal 'chatDebug' that is not compatible with the current version of VS Code"
issue.body: ### Bug details below but to get vscode working again the following WFM: It looks likely publicly the version has already been downgraded for insiders back to 1.111 from a few days ago, installing the insiders version over your existing fixes it download it from: https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders (ie the system installer/user installer). As it looks like 1.112 has been pulled vscode does not try to auto upgrade to it again. ------- Using latest vscode beta combined with pre-release copilot is broken vscode disables it due to: ` This extension is using the API proposal 'chatDebug' that is not compatible with the current version of VS Code.` - Copilot Chat Extension Version: 0.40.2026031001 VS Code Version: 1.112.0-insider (user setup) Commit: eb7e1c7cb89fc44d19bc3d1004ce847869795a38 Date: 2026-03-09T21:05:33-07:00 Electron: 39.8.0 ElectronBuildId: 13470701 Chromium: 142.0.7444.265 Node.js: 22.22.0 V8: 14.2.231.22-electron.0 OS: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200 - Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): ALL - Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): ALL Steps to Reproduce: 1. Use the versions above which seem to be the latest of both (manually checked for updates with both)

issue.body: Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes - VS Code Version: 1.110.1 - OS Version: Fedora Linux 43 Steps to Reproduce: 1. Open VS Code Terminal with fish as your default shell 2. `ssh` to a remote server 3. Type `echo test` 4. Observe as control characters such as space, return, or backspace fail to execute. Alternative steps: 1. Open VS Code Terminal with fish as your default shell 2. Launch `bash` 3. Type `echo test` 4. Observe as control characters such as space, return, or backspace fail to execute.

issue.title: Track loading system certificates with Node.js built-in support

labels.name: engineering
labels.description: VS Code - Build / issue tracking / etc.

labels.name: network
labels.description: Network related issues

issue.body:
Tracking issue for the experimental `http.systemCertificatesNode` setting that enables loading system certificates using Node.js built-in support.

## Background
- Node.js 22+ includes built-in support for loading system certificates
- This is controlled by the `http.systemCertificatesNode` setting (currently defaults to `false`)

## Status
- [ ] Gather telemetry on certificate loading (`additionalCertificates` event)
- [ ] Evaluate enabling by default

## Related
- #277838 - Disable loading system certificates from Node.js by default
- #277064
- microsoft/vscode-internalbacklog#6543

issue.title: I can not use codex agent with my github copilot subscription

issue.title: Support passing a skip reason on TestRun.skipped()

labels.name: feature-request
labels.description: Request for new features or functionality

labels.name: testing
labels.description: Built-in testing support

milestone.title: Backlog
milestone.description: Work not yet planned for a specific release.

issue.body:
When calling `TestRun.errored()` and `TestRun.failed()`, we can pass a `TestMessage | TestMessage[]` to indicate the failure/error.

It would be nice to also be able to provide a message as a skip reason to `TetsRun.skipped()` so that the user can hover over a skipped test and see this reason.

While we can write skip reasons to the normal test output, it can be spammy when looking at the test results, and it won't show when hovering over a skipped test.

issue.body:
When calling `TestRun.errored()` and `TestRun.failed()`, we can pass a `TestMessage | TestMessage[]` to indicate the failure/error.

It would be nice to also be able to provide a message as a skip reason to `TetsRun.skipped()` so that the user can hover over a skipped test and see this reason.

While we can write skip reasons to the normal test output, it can be spammy when looking at the test results, and it won't show when hovering over a skipped test.

issue.title: MCP tool returning structuredContent only leads to error in output view
issue.body: Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: **Yes** !-- 🪓 If you answered No above, use 'Help: Start Extension Bisect' from Command Palette to try to identify the cause. --> Version: 1.110.0-insider (user setup) Commit: d57d4ebe7c1bb4a322a66b714623997fb86c9666 Date: 2026-02-25T05:04:13.210Z Electron: 39.6.0 ElectronBuildId: 13330601 Chromium: 142.0.7444.265 Node.js: 22.22.0 V8: 14.2.231.22-electron.0 OS: Windows_NT x64 10.0.22631 **Steps to Reproduce:** 1. In chat agent mode use MCP tool that _only_ returns `structuredContent`, allow tool execution 2. Observe tool response output view shows error `TypeError: r.content is not iterable` instead of response data **Remarks**: * When tools return both content _and_ structuredContent, output shows both * Other tools like MCPJam can handle MCP tools returning structuredContent only. * MCP spec does allow to return strcturedContent only ("... tool that returns structured content SHOULD also return the serialized JSON in a TextContent block") https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/issues/1624

comment.body: I linked you to the MCP protocol definition. The protocol definition notes that `content` is required, even if it's only an empty array.
